_doc: Unique per-channel content variants (the unique-content engine). Runners prefer their channel's variant over the generic blurb. Strategist generates these for new content per CHANNEL_CONTENT_PLAYBOOK.md. mastodon variant: runner appends url + tags.

threads.text:
Hot take: buying Angi leads is just renting customers you'll lose the second you stop paying. They sell the same lead to 5–12 contractors, so your real cost per BOOKED job is ~$1,430–$2,500. Agree, or am I missing something?

https://booked-job.com/blog/is-angi-worth-it/
chain: Each lead gets sold to 5–12 of you. So your $35 lead is really $35 × the 8 other trucks racing you to the phone.
Stop paying and the leads vanish — you rented an audience instead of building one. What's YOUR cost per booked job? 👇 https://booked-job.com/blog/is-angi-worth-it/

bluesky.text: Ever notice your Angi “leads” already talked to 8 other guys before you called back? That's the model — same lead sold to 5–12 contractors. Real cost per booked job: ~$1,430–$2,500. Free calculator that shows yours 👇
chain: They monetize each homeowner by reselling the request. You're not their customer — you're their inventory.
The fix: own your Google presence + capture the homeowners already on your site. Full math + calculator → booked-job.com/blog/is-angi-worth-it/

mastodon.text: For #HomeService crews weighing lead sites: Angi / HomeAdvisor / Thumbtack sell each lead to 5–12 contractors, so your cost per *booked* job runs ~$1,430–$2,500 vs ~$290 organic. Breakdown + a free cost calculator:

is-angi-worth-it.telegram: 💡 Monday money check: if you buy Angi leads, work out your cost per booked job — not per lead. Most shops are shocked it's $1,400+. Free calculator that does the math 👇 https://booked-job.com/blog/is-angi-worth-it/
is-angi-worth-it.linkedin:
Most contractors think they're losing jobs to a competitor's price. They're losing them to a lead-resale model.

Angi, HomeAdvisor and Thumbtack sell the same lead to 5–12 pros. Factor in real close rates and the cost per *booked* job lands around $1,430–$2,500 — versus ~$290 for a job won through your own reputation.

The deeper problem: the day you stop paying, the pipeline stops. You rented demand instead of building an asset.

The full math (with a calculator): https://booked-job.com/blog/is-angi-worth-it/

threads.text:
Unpopular take: most contractors aren't underpriced because they're cheap. They're underpriced because they confuse markup with margin. A 50% markup is only a 33% margin. That gap is real money on every job. Change my mind 👇

https://booked-job.com/blog/how-to-price-a-job/
chain: A 50% markup feels like 50% profit. It's a 33% margin. That gap compounds across every estimate you write all year.
Price off margin: cost ÷ (1 − target). Most shops have never run that number. Have you? 👇 https://booked-job.com/blog/how-to-price-a-job/

bluesky.text: Quick one for the trades: a “50% markup” is only a 33% margin. If you price off markup, you're undercharging every single job. Fix: cost ÷ (1 − target margin). Free calculator that does it 👇
chain: Quick test: do you price off “cost + X%” or off a target margin? If it's the first, you're probably undercharging.
cost ÷ (1 − margin) is the whole trick. Built a calculator → booked-job.com/blog/how-to-price-a-job/

mastodon.text: Pricing tip for #HomeService owners: markup ≠ margin. A 50% markup is only a 33% margin — which quietly underprices every job. Price off margin instead: cost ÷ (1 − target). Full breakdown + calculator:

how-to-price-a-job.telegram: 💡 Pricing gut-check: a 50% markup is only a 33% margin. If you've been “adding 50%,” you're leaving money on every job. Free margin calculator 👇 https://booked-job.com/blog/how-to-price-a-job/
how-to-price-a-job.linkedin:
The fastest profit win in the trades isn't more leads. It's fixing the estimate.

Most owners confuse markup and margin. A 50% markup sounds like 50% profit — it's actually a 33% margin. Across a year of estimates, that gap is serious money left on the table.

The fix is one formula: price = cost ÷ (1 − target margin).

A calculator that does it for you: https://booked-job.com/blog/how-to-price-a-job/

threads.text:
Hot take: the reason a competitor outranks you on Google isn't SEO magic. It's that they ask for reviews the second the job's done — and you don't. Best time to ask is peak gratitude, then text the link within the hour. Too simple?

https://booked-job.com/blog/get-more-google-reviews/
chain: The shop beating you in the map pack isn't better at SEO. They just ask — every job, while the customer's still standing there.
Ask in person, text the link within the hour, one tap. What's your review count vs theirs? 👇 https://booked-job.com/blog/get-more-google-reviews/

bluesky.text: Service pros: Google reviews decide who shows up in the map pack and who gets the call. Most shops “mean to ask” and never do. The system: ask in person the second the job's done, then text the direct link within the hour. 👇
chain: The shop outranking you usually isn't better at SEO. They just ask — every single job, on the spot.
Ask in person → text the link within the hour → one tap. Full system → booked-job.com/blog/get-more-google-reviews/

mastodon.text: For #HomeService crews: Google reviews are the cheapest marketing you've got. Ask in person at peak gratitude (job just done), then text the direct link within the hour. Ask everyone — review-gating violates Google's policy. Full system:

get-more-google-reviews.telegram:
💡 The cheapest marketing you're ignoring: Google reviews. Ask the second the job's done, then text the direct link within the hour. That's the whole trick. Full ask-system 👇

https://booked-job.com/blog/get-more-google-reviews/
get-more-google-reviews.linkedin:
Reviews are the highest-ROI marketing most home-service shops completely ignore.

They decide who shows up in the map pack and who gets the call — yet most owners “mean to ask” and never build the habit.

What works: ask in person at peak gratitude (the moment the job's done), then text the direct review link within the hour. One tap. Ask every customer — review-gating violates Google's policy.

The full playbook: https://booked-job.com/blog/get-more-google-reviews/

threads.text:
Unpopular take: the slow season isn't a cash-flow problem. It's a planning problem you could see coming in July. Bank a cushion, sell weather-proof work, and — counterintuitively — INCREASE marketing while competitors go quiet. Wrong?

https://booked-job.com/blog/surviving-the-slow-season/
chain: Bank a 3–6 month cushion in the busy months — sweep a fixed % of every job before it feels spendable.
Don't go dark on marketing when it's slow; that's when it's cheapest. How do you handle the lull? 👇 https://booked-job.com/blog/surviving-the-slow-season/

bluesky.text: The trades shops that panic in the slow season are the ones who assumed every busy month would last forever. Win it in the busy season: bank a 3–6mo cushion, sell maintenance plans, and don't go dark on marketing. 👇
chain: Bank a cushion in the busy months and the slow season stops being scary — you can say no to bad jobs.
Keep marketing on when it's quiet; it's cheapest then. Full playbook → booked-job.com/blog/surviving-the-slow-season/

mastodon.text: Cash-flow note for #HomeService owners: the slow season is won in the busy season. Bank a 3–6 month cushion, sell maintenance plans + weather-independent work, re-engage past customers, and keep marketing on while competitors go quiet. Full playbook:

surviving-the-slow-season.telegram:
💡 Slow season is coming — win it now. Bank a 3–6mo cushion, sell maintenance plans, and DON'T cut marketing when it's quiet (competitors do; you shouldn't). Full cash-flow playbook 👇

https://booked-job.com/blog/surviving-the-slow-season/
surviving-the-slow-season.linkedin:
Every trade has a slow stretch. The owners who sail through don't get lucky — they plan in July for what January feels like.

The playbook: bank a 3–6 month cushion during peak months; sell the work that ignores weather (maintenance plans, opposite-season installs); mine past customers (your cheapest pipeline); and counterintuitively, INCREASE marketing while competitors go quiet and ad costs drop.

The full cash-flow playbook: https://booked-job.com/blog/surviving-the-slow-season/
